signals.name: Leopold Aschenbrenner
signals.latest_title: Situational Awareness LP hits $20B AUM, 270% gains in 2026
signals.summary: No new original X posts or essays detected in the last 7 days. Coverage is dominated by third-party reporting on Situational Awareness LP's fund performance — ~270% after fees in 2026 through May, anchored by an early Anthropic stake bought at a ~$60B valuation now worth ~$965B.
signals.connection: Directly relevant to the SpaceX SPCX IPO (Aschenbrenner's infrastructure thesis), frontier AI governance, and markets rally driven by Iran peace talks. His fund's Anthropic bet is live context for the NSA/Mythos story and Trump's Frontier AI EO.

signals.name: Dan Wang
signals.summary: No new posts detected in the last 7 days. His website surfaces evergreen content about US vs. China industrial capacity and the 2026 US semiquincentennial. His book 'Breakneck: China's Quest to Engineer the Future' continues to circulate.
signals.connection: His China tech-industrial lens is highly relevant to the geopolitics and China topic area, especially US-China AI competition underlying the frontier AI EO and export controls debate.

signals.name: Nathan Benaich
signals.latest_title: Air Street Capital Fund III — $232M close
signals.summary: Recent X activity (March 2026) centers on the close of Air Street Fund III at $232,323,232, described as the largest solo GP venture firm in Europe, with commentary that the AI opportunity set has 'accelerated dramatically' and companies are raising faster and larger rounds.
signals.connection: Relevant to frontier tech/AI venture dynamics and AI regulation/governance; his AI-first fund thesis and European VC scaling connect to the broader AI investment boom context.

signals.name: Stanley Druckenmiller
signals.summary: No new interview or public remarks detected in the last 7 days. Most recent notable coverage (May 2026) was Sen. Warren pressing his Duquesne firm to release Kevin Warsh from confidentiality agreements ahead of the Fed chair confirmation vote.
signals.connection: Tangentially relevant to markets and Fed policy: with May CPI at 4.2% and hike bets building into June 17, Druckenmiller's known views on fiscal excess and monetary policy are background context — but no fresh signal this week.

signals.name: Byrne Hobart
signals.latest_title: Anabasis, Memory, Height, Neo-Brandeisians, Canceled, Capacity, Compute, Realism
signals.summary: Published multiple issues of The Diff this week (June 5–6), with topics including compute capacity, P/S ratios in AI, software private equity, automation and utilization, and a thread touching on neo-Brandeisian antitrust. Active cadence continues.
signals.connection: The compute and P/S ratio content speaks directly to SpaceX IPO valuation debates, AI infrastructure buildout, and the SaaS/labor displacement themes. Neo-Brandeisian thread connects to US policy and AI regulation.

signals.name: Michael Burry
signals.latest_title: Trading Post June 8, 2026 — Samsung Electronics at Tangible Book Value
signals.summary: Posted multiple Cassandra Unchained pieces this week: a June 8 post arguing Samsung Electronics should be bought mechanically at tangible book value (a rule that has worked eight times in 30 years), and a June 5 post dissecting Lululemon earnings where tariffs crushed gross margins by 380bps. Also ran a subscriber chat thread on SpaceX's S-1 filing.
signals.connection: Samsung at book value is a semiconductor/AI supply chain call relevant to AI infrastructure themes. Tariff impact on LULU margins directly illustrates the inflation/CPI story (4.2% CPI). SpaceX S-1 chat is live context for today's SPCX IPO.

signals.name: Ben Thompson
signals.latest_title: Power Shifts (Week of June 1, 2026) / SpaceX Hype and the Elon Bargain
signals.summary: Published a full weekly recap covering Google vs. Microsoft competitive dynamics (Google issuing equity to Berkshire), a Satya Nadella interview, and YouTubers disrupting Hollywood. Separately published a piece on the SpaceX IPO, framing it as a return to what an IPO should be — retail investors playing VC — while acknowledging the valuation risk.
signals.connection: The SpaceX IPO analysis is directly on-topic with today's lead story. His Google/Microsoft power-shift framing is core to frontier tech and AI governance. Nadella interview touches AI compute and agentic business models.

signals.name: Paul Graham
signals.summary: Posted a short reflection on June 2 about the value of clarity standards in startup advising at YC — 'their initial answer is a muddy pool, then we make it clear.' No new essays detected in the last 7 days.
signals.connection: Light relevance to labor/workforce and AI-era startup formation themes, but no direct connection to this week's top stories.

signals.name: Benedict Evans
signals.summary: Published a substantive essay (May 24) arguing that attempts to score job exposure to AI are 'mostly impossible' — job titles change, business models change beneath stable titles, and historical census data is too noisy to extrapolate. His Spring 2026 'AI eats the world' deck is also circulating widely.
signals.connection: Directly relevant to Labor & Workforce topic area. The AI job exposure essay is essential context for the debate around frontier AI EO labor implications and AI-driven productivity claims.

signals.name: Packy McCormick
signals.latest_title: Westmag out of stealth — America needs a motor and actuator company
signals.summary: On June 2, announced his investment in Westmag, a stealth motor and actuator company now public, writing a deep dive on why America needs domestic capability in this hardware layer. Frames it as part of his 'Electric Slide' techno-industrial thesis.
signals.connection: Connects to US industrial policy, defense/energy supply chain, and the applied tech theme. Motor and actuator manufacturing is foundational to robotics and defense applications — relevant to defense & security and energy topic areas.

signals.name: CJ Gustafson
signals.latest_title: SpaceX S-1 breakdown — three businesses on one ticker
signals.summary: Published a detailed breakdown of the SpaceX S-1: Starlink at $11.4B 2025 revenue / 63% EBITDA margins; the launch business at $4.1B / ~breakeven controlling ~85% of global launches; and xAI+X at $3.2B revenue / ($6.4B) operating loss burning over $1B/month. Notes restated financials under common-control accounting due to SpaceX's acquisition of xAI.
signals.connection: Directly on-topic with today's SPCX IPO lead story. His financial granularity on Starlink vs. xAI+X burn is the cleanest public breakdown of the IPO structure available this week.

signals.name: Lenny Rachitsky
signals.latest_title: Reaction to Anthropic's 8x engineering output stat
signals.summary: Posted 'Wild' on June 4 in response to Anthropic's claim that engineers now ship 8x as much code per quarter vs. 2021–2025. Also promoted a Benedict Evans podcast episode on AI being like '1997 for the internet.' Pinned post promotes a Tony Fadell interview.
signals.connection: The 8x engineering productivity stat is live ammunition for the Labor & Workforce and Frontier Tech topic areas, and connects to the NSA/Mythos offensive cyber story (AI-accelerated code generation in sensitive contexts).

signals.name: Andrej Karpathy
signals.summary: Announced on May 19 that he joined Anthropic's pretraining team, calling the next few years at the frontier of LLMs 'especially formative.' He is leading a team using Claude to accelerate pretraining research — what Fortune called 'the Karpathy Loop' of AI-driven self-improvement in training.
signals.connection: Highly relevant to frontier AI governance, the Trump Frontier AI EO (voluntary 30-day government preview), and the NSA/Mythos story — Karpathy now sits on the pretraining team of the very lab whose model is reportedly being used by NSA.

signals.name: Ilya Sutskever
signals.summary: No new posts detected in the last 7 days. Most recent notable public statement (Feb 2026) praised Anthropic and OpenAI for 'not backing down' in an unspecified challenging situation. He is now CEO of Safe Superintelligence Inc. following Daniel Gross's departure.
signals.connection: SSI's safe superintelligence mission is background context for the frontier AI EO and AI governance topic area, but no fresh signal this week.

signals.name: Noam Brown
signals.latest_title: Insights from Large-Scale Inference Computing — benchmarks ignore reasoning budget
signals.summary: Published a lengthy article (June 8, 755K views) arguing that all AI benchmark leaderboards are misleading because they fail to disclose inference compute cost — the same model given $1 vs. $10,000 to think produces vastly different scores. Calls for compute-normalized benchmarks as the only logical path forward.
signals.connection: Directly relevant to frontier AI governance and the Trump Frontier AI EO: if government preview programs rely on benchmark comparisons to assess model capability, Brown's argument that those benchmarks are systematically misleading is a critical governance gap. Also connects to the NSA/Mythos story — compute budget is a hidden variable in offensive cyber capability assessments.

signals.name: François Chollet
signals.summary: No posts detected in the last 7 days. The most recent substantive activity was the ARC-AGI-3 developer preview release (January 2026), a fully interactive benchmark requiring AIs to learn novel games on the fly through trial and error — designed to resist memorization and test true generalization.
signals.connection: His ARC-AGI-3 benchmark is directly relevant to the AI governance and frontier AI EO topic: if government model previews use static benchmarks, Chollet's work highlights why those evaluations may miss generalization capability — echoing Noam Brown's inference-compute critique from a different angle.
